content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 210–211 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_013_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_013_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### When a Prophet Leads You Astray (vv. 1–5)

**1** [In Hebrew tradition, this verse is 13:2] Suppose a prophet or a dreamer of dreams appears among you and offers you a sign or a wonder — **2** and the sign or wonder actually comes true — and then he says to you, "Come, let us follow other gods (gods you have not known before) and worship them." **3** Do not listen to that prophet or that dreamer. The LORD your God is testing you to find out whether you love him with all your heart and with all your soul. **4** The LORD your God is the one you must follow. Fear him alone. Keep his commands. Listen to his voice. Serve him and hold fast to him. **5** But that prophet or dreamer must be put to death, because he has urged you to turn away from the LORD your God who rescued you from Egypt and redeemed you from that place of slavery. He was trying to drive you off the road that the LORD your God commanded you to walk. Purge the evil from among you.

---

### When a Family Member Entices You (vv. 6–11)

**6** Your own brother — the son of your mother — or your son or your daughter, or the wife you hold dear, or your closest friend, may whisper to you secretly and say, "Let's go and worship other gods" — gods that neither you nor your ancestors have ever known, **7** gods of the peoples surrounding you, whether nearby or far away, from one end of the earth to the other. **8** Do not agree with them. Do not listen to them. Show them no pity. Do not spare them. Do not cover for them. **9** You yourself must be first to raise your hand against them to execute them, and then the whole community follows. **10** Stone them to death, because they tried to pull you away from the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the place of slavery. **11** All Israel will hear what has happened and be terrified, and no one will ever dare to do such an evil thing among you again.

---

### When a Whole Town Is Led Astray (vv. 12–18)

**12** Suppose you hear a report about one of the towns the LORD your God is giving you to live in — **13** that some scoundrels from your own community have gone out and corrupted the people of their town, saying, "Let's go and worship other gods" (gods you have never known before). **14** You must investigate the matter thoroughly. Question people carefully and search it out. If the report is true and proven — if this abomination really has occurred among you — **15** you must put every inhabitant of that town to the sword. Destroy it completely, along with all its people and livestock. **16** Gather all the plunder into the middle of the open square and burn the whole town — all its plunder as well — as a burnt offering to the LORD your God. Let it remain a heap of ruins forever. Never rebuild it. **17** Do not let any of the devoted things cling to your hands. Then the LORD will turn from his fierce anger, show you compassion, and increase your numbers, just as he swore to your ancestors — **18** as long as you listen to the voice of the LORD your God, keeping all his commands that I am giving you today, and doing what is right in his eyes.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — Hebrew chapter numbering**: The Hebrew Bible numbers this verse 13:2, so the chapter effectively begins with what English Bibles call v. 1. PNEUMA follows English verse numbering throughout.

**v. 1 — "dreamer of dreams"**: Hebrew *chalom chalomot*. Dynamic rendering: "a dreamer of dreams" preserves the idiom but the import is clear — someone claiming divine revelation through sleep visions. The ancient world took such figures seriously; Moses is insisting the test of a prophet is loyalty to YHWH, not supernatural accuracy.

**vv. 1–3 — The counter-intuitive test**: PNEUMA highlights the shock of this passage: even if the miracle comes true, that is not sufficient validation. The LORD permits false wonders to test the heart. This is theologically radical — signs and wonders are not the ultimate criterion of truth.

**v. 3 — "find out whether you love him"**: Hebrew *yodea'* (knowing/testing). Dynamic choice: "find out" captures the purpose of divine testing without implying God lacks knowledge — it is a relational proving, not divine ignorance.

**v. 5 — "drive you off the road"**: Hebrew *lehadi'ach* (to push aside, to thrust away). Dynamic: "drive you off the road" — vivid, concrete. The covenant path is pictured as a road; the false prophet as a force pushing travelers into the wilderness.

**v. 5 — "Purge the evil from among you"**: Hebrew *uvi'arta hara' miqirbecha*. This refrain recurs throughout Deuteronomy like a structural drumbeat. PNEUMA renders it consistently with force — the community is responsible for its own moral health.

**v. 6 — "the wife you hold dear"**: Hebrew *eshet cheiqecha* (wife of your bosom). Dynamic: "the wife you hold dear" — captures the emotional intimacy without archaic body imagery. The list of relationships deliberately moves from distant (brother) to most intimate (dearest friend), intensifying the horror of the command.

**v. 8 — "Do not cover for them"**: Hebrew *lo' tikasseh 'alav* (do not conceal). Dynamic: "do not cover for them" — the language of complicity and shielding. Loyalty to God overrides the natural human instinct to protect family.

**v. 9 — "You yourself must be first"**: Hebrew *yadcha tihyeh bo barishonah* (your hand shall be first against him). The accuser must also be the first executioner — a safeguard against false accusation through personal cost.

**v. 13 — "scoundrels"**: Hebrew *anashim b'nei b'liya'al* (sons of Belial / worthlessness). Dynamic: "scoundrels" — captures the moral dismissal. "Sons of Belial" is the Hebrew idiom for characterless, destructive people.

**vv. 15–16 — "devoted to destruction"**: Hebrew *cherem* — the ban of total destruction. PNEUMA does not retain "devoted things" as a technical term here but renders the concept through narrative action: total burning, permanent ruin. The town itself becomes a kind of altar — all consumed.

**v. 17 — "fierce anger"**: Hebrew *charon appo* (the burning of his nose/anger). Dynamic: "fierce anger" — the anthropomorphic idiom is preserved in meaning. God's passion for exclusive loyalty is not cold policy but burning covenant love. — *PNEUMA*
